data.title: Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical: The Market Is Overlooking 2026 Catalysts
data.description:
It has been nearly a year and a half since my last Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (RARE) article, where I discussed the company’s Q3 earnings report that showed a 42% year-over-year revenue increase, which encouraged me to finally commit to a starter position in RARE. I believed that the company’s late-stage pipeline could provide multiple catalysts for future growth, including Setrusumab and DTX401. At that time, Ultragenyx had a strong cash position, and I saw a path to profitability by 2026, which could allow the company to avoid the need for extensive dilutive financing. Since then, RARE is down roughly 50% after the company received a complete response letter (CRL) from the FDA for UX111 in Sanfilippo syndrome type A, then Setrusumab for osteogenesis imperfecta failed to hit the primary endpoint in two late-stage trials, and the company’s guidance fell short of the consensus. As a result, I decided to hold off on adding to my position.

However, I was reading a recent investors conference transcript and realized that my Ultragenyx thesis is still intact, as the company leverages their commercial products (Crysvita, Dojolvi, Mepsevii, and Evkeeza) to provide a financial base to help fund their impressive late-stage pipeline that could move the company closer to profitability. It appears that 2026 is going to be a pivotal year for Ultragenyx with multiple data readouts, regulatory milestones, and potential approvals that could bolster investor sentiment and improve RARE’s long-term prospects. As a result, I’m keeping a close eye on the ticker for an opportunity to add to my dormant position.

I intend to review the company’s current commercial foundation and the list of potential catalysts on the horizon. Then, I will take a look at the commercial implications of these programs and their potential impact on the company’s valuation. In addition, I will point out a few downside risks that investors should be aware of when managing their RARE position. Finally, I take a look at the daily chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add to my position.

Underrated Commercial Foundation

When catching up on Ultragenyx’s current condition, I was really surprised that a rare disease biotech pulled in $673M last year, which was up 20% from 2024. Digging deeper, it looks as if Crysvita was responsible for $481M of that $673M. Meanwhile, Dojolvi brought in $96M, Mepsevii $37M, and Evkeeza experienced 84% growth year-over-year to log $59M.

So, I would say that Ultragenyx is capable of handling multiple launches in different rare disease arenas while still pushing their late-stage programs closer to the finish line. This commercial groundwork could provide enough stability for the company to make their push towards their goal of profitability by 2027.

Catalyst Timeline

Ultragenyx has several potent clinical catalysts slated in 2026 and 2027, including readouts from their Angelman syndrome program, Glycogen Storage Disease Type Ia (GSDIa), and Sanfilippo syndrome, as well as Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency and Wilson’s disease.

GTX-102 is Ultragenyx’s Angelman syndrome program and is one of their most anticipated catalysts, with two Phase III trials. It is important to note that Angelman syndrome does not have an approved therapy that has disease-modifying ability. So, there is a big opportunity for Ultragenyx to be the first mover and expand the market. In fact, one analyst believes that GTX-102 could have peak sales of $1.2B.

DTX401 is Ultragenyx’s gene therapy for GSDIa, which has a PDUFA date set in August 2026 after their Phase III trial showed improved glucose control and reduced dietary dependence. A Bank of America analyst has DTX401’s peak sales at about $796M, which would be impressive for therapy coming from a population of roughly 6K patients.

Ultragenyx’s UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome could be another breakthrough drug because there is a lack of approved therapies. UX11’s cognitive and biomarker marks still have my approval. The company announced that the FDA has set a PDUFA date for September 19th, 2026.

The company’s DTX301 for the OTC program targets a rare urea cycle disorder, while UX701 targets Wilson’s disease. Both programs are in Phase III or are in pre-pivotal stages, with potentially important readouts over the next 12 months.

I would suggest reviewing the point above quickly one more time and understanding that we are looking at multiple Ultragenyx programs that are approaching FDA approval with breakthrough therapy designations. So, we could see Ultragenyx report approval across multiple programs, which could lead to multiple launches over the next few years, resulting in significant revenue growth going into the next decade.

Conservative Commercial Implications

I will concede that attempting to determine peak sales for rare disease and gene therapy products is incredibly murky because we don't really know what the market is for the product until it actually hits the market. We can look at patient populations and the market size to estimate the commercial opportunity, but penetration and prices are always a big question mark because these therapies are novel, so we don't know if there is demand for patients to try something completely new, and we don't know if payers are going to fully support the therapy. So, I tend to be cautious when trying to determine the valuation of rare disease pipelines.

I’m going to attempt to calculate very conservative peak estimates for Ultragenyx’s late-stage programs.

When trying to determine Ultragenyx’s late-stage pipeline valuation, I will be modeling using only the U.S. market with a 30% peak penetration and a conservative estimate of pricing based on other established rare-disease products. Under this conservative approach, I calculated that Ultragenyx has an aggregate $1.5B-$2.4B in peak annual revenue potential from these programs in the United States alone. So, not counting for ex-U.S. expansion or a broader label penetration.

GTW-102 for Angelman syndrome looks to be the largest single opportunity in the U.S. pipeline. With an estimated 6K to 10K treatable patients when I adjust for clinical eligibility constraints and only 30% peak penetration at an estimated $300K per patient (Spinraza is around $350K per year), I get a peak revenue range of $540M to $900M.

The second-largest opportunity could be DTX301 with OTC deficiency, with 800 to 1.2K treatable U.S. patients, and assuming a $1.5M one-time gene therapy price (Zolgensma is $2.1M) and 30% penetration, which gives us peak sales of $360M to $540M. DTX401 could add $210M to $300M in U.S. peak sales, assuming 350 to 500 eligible patients and a $2M price tag. In addition, UX701 could give Ultragenyx 5K-8K treatable U.S. patients with chronic therapies around $125K per year, which would be about $190M-$300M in peak sales at 30% penetration.

UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome might be the smallest in absolute U.S. population, but the lack of approved disease-modifying therapies could make it a lucrative opportunity for Ultragenyx. With approximately 250 to 600 eligible U.S. patients and applying 30% penetration with a $2M price tag (Zolgensma comp), we get a peak sales range from $150M to $360M.

I know these estimates aren't very rosy, but I'm deliberately modeling them with some bearish assumptions. I did this to illustrate that Ultragenyx still has a lot to gain even if all of these programs achieve modest penetration, which could still yield a multi-billion-dollar expansion in their revenue base over time.

I’m not the only one with an optimistic outlook. It appears Street analysts are also expecting the company to report strong double-digit growth over the next 10 years, going from roughly $770M in 2026 to about $3.75B in 2035.

What is more, Street analysts are projecting Ultragenyx to report similar EPS growth, going from -4.40 in 2026 to 12.21 in 2035.

The industry’s average price-to-sales is roughly 4x-5x, so assuming Ultragenyx were to pull in roughly $3.75B in 2035, we could be looking at about $152 per share. RARE is currently trading around $23 per share, so there is significant upside if all goes well for Ultragenyx over the next 10 years.

Risks to Consider

Certainly, Ultragenyx has significant downside risks to consider when managing your position. I would first highlight the hallmark risks that come with being a rare disease company. We have to recognize that some of these programs are targeting very small patient populations, so a small network of treatment centers might impact how quickly patients can get to a center and be treated. So, it is possible that we see some of these programs get off to a slow start, despite there being a clear need.

Another risk to consider is that some of Ultragenyx’s programs are gene therapies, which carry plenty of risks, including the complexity of administration. AAV-based gene therapies often require the patient to go through pre-screening, preconditioning of their immune system, and post-treatment monitoring. Just adding these steps could limit site capacity and throughput. So, even if there is demand, it is possible that Ultragenyx’s therapies won't be able to scale fast enough early in the commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge that the rare disease addressable market can be impacted by a lag in diagnosis and referrals. As a result, the real-world treatable population could be much smaller than the estimated market derived from prevalence statistics. The company can launch education and support programs to help improve genetic testing and newborn screening, but they are still relying on other parties to execute.

Perhaps the most concerning is the payer dynamics of rare diseases, which can add a lot of friction. Even when we are dealing with orphan indications, payers appear to be reluctant to really support these therapies. Yes, rare disease therapies pose a big risk for payers because they are flipping the bill for therapies with big price tags but limited real-world data. So, even if Ultragenyx gets these therapies approved, payers might require a prior authorization, ask for restrictive eligibility, and require a reimbursement agreement. Any of those hurdles could delay access to the company’s therapies and hurt the early stages of a commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge the potential threat from competition. Although most of Ultragenyx’s programs have limited competition, we have to anticipate that a competitor will challenge these products at some point down the road. This could force Ultragenyx to adjust their prices substantially, which might result in a significant loss in revenue.

I also want to stress that having so many potent catalysts on the docket could lead to elevated volatility as the market attempts to digest the likelihood of approval and commercial success. RARE has a history of the share price being essentially cut in half following weak data or regulatory setbacks. So, investors need to accept that their position could have big swings in value over the next couple of years as the company’s current pipeline attempts to get through the FDA. Keep in mind that any setback or failure could generate doubts about the company’s financial health and timeline to break even. The company did finish 2025 with $680M in cash, but that cash position could quickly evaporate as the company maintains several late-stage programs and commercial launch prep. Plus, the company has $1.25B in debt, so the threat of dilution could grow if the company doesn’t have a strong winning percentage over the next 2 years.

Last but not least, Ultragenyx might have to deal with regulatory uncertainty and macroeconomic factors. Healthcare costs continue to rise, and scrutiny of the regulatory approval process for novel treatment options might lead to harsh ramifications for Ultragenyx's future operations.

Despite these risks, I'm maintaining my RARE conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio

My Plan

As I mentioned in my introduction, my RARE position has been gathering dust for over a year now, but the recent sell-off has enticed me to consider picking up some shares at these prices ahead of some potent catalysts.

Looking at the RARE Daily Chart, we see a potential double-bottom setup forming along with a bullish divergence on the RSI while showing strength. Plus, we can see the share price has climbed above the proximal anchored-VWAP and is now neutral on the Go-No-Go indicator while pressing the top of the Keltner Channel.

Indeed, the share price will still have to deal with the 200-Day EMA, and the longstanding anchored VWAP above, but I would say some bullish signals are starting to light up. So, I will look to add in the immediate term.

Once I have added, I will switch up my RARE game plan to focus on catalyst-driven opportunities to manage my position.

Admittedly, I will not hesitate to book profits on spikes in the share price following clinical or commercial updates. My goal is to trade this position in order to generate profit and construct a "house money" core position that has been funded by the market to hold as a long-term investment.

data.title: Gambling.com: SEO Recovery Appears Underway
data.description:
It's been more than a few months since my last article on Gambling.com Group (GAMB), and quite a few developments have occurred:

  • FY-25 results
  • CEO change
  • SEO recovery (partially)
  • Insider buys

We will discuss all of these developments in the article, but I want to first highlight how disconnected GDG's stock is from reality. The company's market cap currently stands at $125 million. For perspective, the company's historical and projected FCFs are as follows:

  • 2021: $13.7 million
  • 2022: $18.4 million
  • 2023: $17.5 million
  • 2024: $36.3 million
  • 2025: $36.3 million
  • 2026: $39.6 million (expected based on last year's FCF margin and 2026 revenue guidance)

In other words, if the company stopped growing today, it could theoretically buy back every single share in three years. That said, readers familiar with the company know there are meaningful debt burdens preventing GDG from executing large buybacks in the near term (2026-2028). Still, the point stands that the company is being valued as if it is going out of business, when in reality, revenue and profits are hitting record highs each year. The company's FCF makes up such a substantial portion of its market cap that it is truly unbelievable to me how the price can be so low.

Management appears to agree. The (now former) CEO, Charles Gillespie, recently disclosed that insiders purchased over 500k shares in the open market, representing roughly 1.5% of the total float, with him leading the charge, buying 351k of those shares.

At the end of the day, GDG is a company growing at double digits with double-digit FCF margins. Conservatively forecasting GDG's future FCF leads me to believe the company is worth about $17 per share.

Current State of the Business

Rather than rehash numbers, I want to focus on a higher level. We will also start with the bear case.

GDG enters 2026 as a business in transition, with two distinct segments: marketing and sports data. The marketing side of the business has come under significant (although 'overblown' is a better word) pressure, specifically in regard to SEO marketing. Organic search, historically a high-margin growth driver, has become less reliable due to a combination of Google algorithm instability, increased competition (including AI-driven spam), and evolving user behavior. Former CEO Charles Gillespie repeatedly described this as "business as usual," expecting SEO performance to normalize in subsequent quarters.

That quarter never came.

I wouldn't say there is a structural impairment in GDG's SEO channel yet, but if this trend continues throughout 2026, I might concede. That said, while SEO has struggled, management has done an exceptional job scaling alternative paid marketing channels to offset lost revenue. They don't get enough credit for the speed at which this was executed. To put this into perspective, marketing revenue still grew while organic traffic on some flagship domains declined by as much as 80% YoY. The tradeoff, however, is margin compression, as paid channels are structurally lower margin than SEO (gross margins declined to 91% in 2025 vs. 94% in 2024 and 100% in years past).

The other segment, sports data, is both highly profitable and growing, but it is essentially an acquired business following GDG's $160 million purchase of Odds Holdings in 2025. GDG would like you to believe this is the future of the company, which could ultimately prove true, but at this moment in time, sports data is not scaling anywhere near the level required to justify its acquisition price or investor belief that it's the future of the company.

Odds Holding makes up 25% of GDG revenue and is only growing in the high teens, which is less than marketing's historical growth, meaning that sports data can mathematically never make up more than 25% of GDG revenue unless marketing declines significantly or sports data experiences exponential growth, for which 'high teens' does not qualify.

Turning to the balance sheet, we can see a picture of a highly leveraged company. The company possesses $16.4 million in cash and $192 million in liabilities. While this appears insurmountable at first glance, the company's FCF is sufficient enough to deleverage the balance sheet over 3-5 years.

Future State of the Business

Not all is woe at GDG. In fact, there is an immense potential upside if the business continues to stabilize and even resumes moderate growth.

Here are some positives:

  1. For the first time in the company's history, SEO revenue made up less than half of overall marketing revenue, signaling less dependence on one channel.
  2. When accounting for marketing revenue shares and sports data subscriptions, over 50% of GDG's revenue is recurring.
  3. Prediction markets offer a potential growth avenue as they significantly expand the TAM across both marketing and sports data. Prediction markets create new demand for odds data, trading tools, and consumer education.
  4. In the year the company's search traffic collapsed, management still grew the marketing segment. At this point, the worst is past us, as there is little search traffic left to lose.

For 2026, the company expects revenue in the range of $170 million to $180 million, representing 3-9% YoY growth. My gut feeling is management sandbagged guidance to avoid their Q3-25 fiasco, where they had to cut guidance down from $175 million to $165 million, resulting in a huge sell-off of the stock.

When asked why guidance appears so subdued, CEO Charles Gillespie effectively admitted the company is prioritizing conservatism, stating they are "very conscious of putting out numbers that we can hit even if none of those things outperform," and later adding, "That's the idea… we want to be pretty conservative."

Speaking of Charles Gillespie, GDG has a new CEO at the helm: co-founder Kevin McCrystle. The transition takes place this May, after 20 years of CEO service from Gillespie, who will become appointed Executive Chairman of the Board. I think it's great the new CEO is the co-founder, who, unlike an external CEO, has much more natural motivation to continue growing the business.

Valuation

Let's take a look at a reasonable DCF, incorporating a few recent developments:

  • Partial SEO recovery
  • The World Cup
  • Prediction Markets

Revenue

I forecast 10% revenue growth for the next decade. This is essentially the top of their (sandbagged) guidance for 2026. I believe 10% is conservative for two reasons:

  1. The company's historical revenue growth rate is 40.73% over the past seven years (though primarily acquisition-driven).

  2. SEO trends appear to be stabilizing, which should support a return to marketing growth.

Partial SEO Recovery

GDG's flagship domain, gambling.com, experienced a massive decline in organic traffic during 2025, down roughly 80% since its peak in the summer of 2024. However, since the end of February 2026, organic traffic has shown some life again, as we can see in the above chart. Gambling.com hit a peak of 500k of organic traffic, and as former CEO Gillespie importantly stated, SEO conversion rates remained stable throughout the decline, meaning traffic recovery should translate directly into revenue recovery.

In some serious positive news, RotoWire appears to be killing it. Organic search traffic is at ATHs, crossing 7 million visitors, and recently, Craig Cummings, VP of GDG, posted that RotoWire is the biggest casino affiliate YTD 2026, according to The Business of iGaming.

As a general note, RotoWire revenue is captured in the sports data segment, not the marketing segment, but either way, this rapid growth should help contribute to revenue growth.

Looking ahead, GDG is likely to capture two large opportunities: the World Cup and prediction markets. The former should drive sports betting activity, while the latter expands demand for data products. GDG also became the first provider of proprietary sportsbook data API inside Claude. While this doesn't necessarily translate to immediate revenue growth, it does create a small moat and competitive advantage (first-mover advantage).

Note: Semrush seems to be more generous with the way it measures organic traffic. AhRefs is more conservative, but I don't have access to its data.

Operating Margins

I set operating margins to be 20% to reflect the increased cost of diversifying marketing channels. For perspective, the company's operating margins were 21.37% in 2025 and averaged 26.81% over the past nine years. When compared to the nine-year average, I essentially baked in a 25% margin of safety in the margins.

Other Assumptions

All other assumptions were set on historical averages, and the WACC was calculated to be 8.8%. The terminal rate is set to 2% to conservatively keep pace with inflation.

If my forecasts prove accurate, the stock is worth almost $17 per share, representing a 368% return from today's severely depressed levels of $3.61 per share.

Risks

Gambling.com Group carries numerous risks for potential investors. The following list is meant to highlight select important risks but is non-exhaustive.

Credibility & Execution Risk

Management has consistently guided towards a return to growth in the marketing segment, yet results have materially lagged those expectations. If management continues to misguide (i.e., with their late 2025 downward guidance revision), the more investors will lose faith in them.

In fact, I would argue that skepticism is already taking place as I write this, as it seems many funds have sold out of their positions and may not re-enter without proven clarity from management.

Unclear Near-Term Growth Drivers

State-level legalization of online gambling and sports betting is slow and unpredictable, thus making contributions from new markets sporadic. The market rewards consistency, and the natural unpredictability of new cash flows in this industry acts as a lid on the stock price. Additionally, the company's specific plan/strategy around capitalizing on prediction markets is unknown.

Google Dependency

Despite meaningful progress, GDG still services a significant portion of marketing revenue from Google-driven traffic. While this SEO dependency has declined to a little under half of marketing revenue, it remains a key risk. Continued volatility in search ranking and algorithm changes could materially impact performance until diversification efforts and the sports data segment represent a larger share of the business.

AI

There was speculation last year that AI would cannibalize a lot of Google search inquiries. However, this was proven false as Google search queries actually increased. I currently don't view AI as a threat to GDG, as they will continue to integrate APIs into LLMs, and the intent behind AI queries and Search queries is different. This is a rapidly evolving and dynamic space, though, and thus, this risk should be kept top of mind by investors.

Regulatory Risk

The online gambling industry is highly regulated (recent developments in Finland highlight how quickly policy shifts can impact companies such as Gambling.com). Any future tightening or changing of regulations could weigh on both revenue and profitability.

Swish Lawsuit

Another layer of uncertainty stems from the ongoing $100 million Swish Analytics litigation against Odds Holding related to data scraping. The outcome of the lawsuit, or any indemnification from the original owners of Odds Holding, is unclear and needs to be monitored. An adverse outcome would be a significant cash flow drain on GDG and could delay its share price appreciation.

Takeaway

GDG is a company that needs a few years to sort out its balance sheet and restore some confidence in its marketing business. During that time, if the company can continue growing the sports data segment, it should emerge in 2029 with a clean balance sheet, plenty of FCF, and thus, the ability to cannibalize its own shares.

I conservatively value the company at $17 based on the forecasts written in this piece. As always with an investment, investors must weigh opportunity cost, risk, and reward. In GDG's case, I believe the reward could be immense, but the opportunity cost of waiting three years and risk profile may not be preferential or suitable, respectively, for some investors.

data.title: Spartan Delta: Putting The Success Odds In Your Favor
data.description:
Many years ago, when I first began writing articles for the website, there was a huge interest in having me write about energy stocks that did not separate investors from their money, as is extremely common in this industry. The very extreme case was a stock I wrote about years ago that quadrupled in price one week before it filed for bankruptcy (roughly). The other thing I wrote about was "The Big Discovery Trap" which very often happens with very small companies. Spartan Delta (DALXF) has long been a way to avoid a lot of the "traps" in this industry, as the last article noted.

The Record

This management has long been known for building and then selling substantial parts of the company to essentially begin over again.

A company like this one is suitable for new investors in the energy industry because this management has a fairly long history of success, as shown above.

Probably the most interesting takeaway from managements like this one is that many investors believe that a lot of debt is necessary for a "homerun" in this part of the stock market. But most of the managements like this one that are very successful generally keep the debt levels to a very conservative level. Unlike a very common investor attitude, most managements consider debt a very seldom used tool.

But the major idea is that successful (or "great") investors often center upon repeatable results (and consistency). Whereas much of the time I run into the attitude of "How do I get every last penny?"

Rather than get every last penny from the current idea, I tend to look for results, as shown above, that are far more likely to avoid big losses.

Logan Energy

As is often the case with professionals that build and sell (either companies or parts of companies), this management decided that a certain proposal was different enough that a separate company, Logan Energy (LOECF) was formed and spun off. Both companies are Canadian operators that report using Canadian dollars. The only difference is the location chosen for operations.

Evidently, management considered the location considerations different enough to justify a separate company. But again, the same management shown above that made all those returns to shareholders also runs this company.

That puts the odds in favor of the investor when many similar-sized companies will not be around a few years later.

The Business

As was noted in the annual earnings press release in February, the remaining operations had the main goal of producing from an interval that produced rich gas. These leases had long produced dry gas. But newer production techniques due to advancing technology had now made a different interval competitive.

As a result of this shift in emphasis, oil production grew tremendously, as shown above. That is very likely to lower the corporate breakeven point in the long run as well as increase profitability.

Map Of Operations

This company is at the rapidly growing stage. Generally, whenever a decision is made to sell some operations or spin off some part of the company into a new company, there is a beginning budget made.

As of right now, there would appear to be a budget that will keep production level with the past fiscal year. But there is also a goal to increase the more valuable liquids production as the natural gas production decreases.

That means that even though production per share may not increase, profitability is very likely to increase.

It can be a bit scary to watch experienced management "slap a company together" rapidly because oftentimes, in the beginning, cash flow is very negative. But a management as successful as this one knows what it is doing and has a final goal in mind.

Most of these managements will switch to a "live within means" model that may or may not include a dividend in the future. Therefore, out-of-control cash spending is generally not the issue here as it is elsewhere.

Nonetheless, it does not hurt to watch the debt levels "just in case".

Summary

A company like Spartan Delta is one that is suitable for a wide variety of investors. However, new investors may want to focus on a company like this one to learn about the industry in a relatively safe way. This management is going to do a lot of things right that can then be used to compare against other industry ideas.

What further simplifies things is that generally a stock like this can be held until management sells the company. This helps beginning investors to determine when the time comes to buy the industry and then when to sell the industry.

When a management like this one jumps back into the industry (as several have in other articles I wrote), then obviously it is time to consider purchasing shares of companies. Similarly, when a management sells part or all of the company, as was done a couple of years back, then it is time to see if that was a sell signal by people that really know the industry.

In the rare event that the stock price clearly gets ahead of itself, it can be sold and then repurchased on the inevitable pullback. Right now, we seem to be far away from an overpriced stock, though.

The finances here are rock solid with a very conservative debt level. That is typical for a management with this level of experience.

Risks

A severe and sustained commodity price downturn might well cause me to re-evaluate the prospects of the company. But it should also be considered that the conservative debt level combined with management experience would likely cause a lot less suffering here than is the case elsewhere in the industry.

One of the reasons for the low debt levels is the very volatile stock prices. Future stock prices are also considered to have very low visibility. That makes financial leverage extra dangerous. Instead, a management like this is likely to go for an operational advantage by finding above-average geology to work with. Then the company will be far better suited to withstanding an extremely nasty cyclical downturn.

Similarly, there are many companies that announce a "big discovery". But North America is one of the most explored continents in the world. Therefore, the location of the oil is often known by the industry well ahead of any "discovery" announcement. Rather than relying upon reserves, investors need to concentrate on production costs, cash flow, and amounts recoverable. Often times, smaller companies' "big discovery announcements" are followed up with disappointing production results (and high costs).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iran: Not Close To Over Yet
data.description:
Thanks, mostly to Wednesday's big rally, the major indices had their best week since November. The S&P 500 added 3.6% this week, the Nasdaq climbed 4.7%, and the Dow, advanced three percent. With last week's rise, all the major indexes are now close to where they started 2026, despite the huge uptick in volatility.

Last week's trading action was a great example of how complacent investors have become despite growing threats to the U.S. economy and equity markets. The big rise in stocks last week was triggered by a two-week ceasefire announced at the 11th hour Tuesday between the U.S. and Iran. To call this ceasefire 'fragile' is overly generous. The agreement supposedly consisted of a cessation of hostilities combined with the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

There is already a divergence of opinion between the U.S. and Iran on whether this ceasefire included Lebanon, where Israel has launched their biggest bombing missions in decades in order to root out Iran's proxy Hezbollah. And very few ships have gone through the Strait of Hormuz, with only a handful of Iran‑linked and/or Chinese vessels transiting this chokepoint. An insignificant number, given over 800-900 vessels are backed up waiting to go through this transit point. Roughly half of which are oil tankers.

And while talks in Pakistan will be the highest-level face-to-face talks between Tehran and Washington in years, given the apparent gulf between the two sides, it is hard to be overly optimistic of a permanent resolution being reached. My own view is that it is basically a coin flip which way these discussions play out.

I could easily see these discussions leading to nowhere. Both sides will use the two-week ceasefire to rearm, and hostilities will break out again as soon as the ceasefire period ends, if not before. And that scenario is not priced into the markets. Interestingly, the oil markets, after posting their biggest daily decline in six years on Wednesday, did little the rest of the week. One would expect oil would have plunged further if investors believed a long-term solution was in the offing. 10-year Treasury yields also should have dropped if there was optimism around peace talks, but that simply hasn't happened. Therefore, my view is markets believe this conflict isn't nearly over yet.

And even if peace broke out next week, a lot of damage has already been done to both the U.S. and global economy. It may be already too late to avoid a recession or at least a bout of stagflation. And these signs are increasingly present. March consumer sentiment plunged last week to historical lows, with one-year inflation expectations rising a full 100 bps from the previous month to 4.8%.

And speaking of fragile, there was a cacophony of voices noting the increasing probabilities of recession and/or stagflation for the U.S. economy last week. Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, stated that recession risks have become “uncomfortably high” as the U.S. economy is now “very fragile” and teetering on the edge of a downturn. Mr. Zandi also unveiled a new recession indicator he dubbed the “Vicious Cycle Index,” which is currently already pointing to a recession. This indicator is basically the classic Sahm Rule modified to account for labor participation rates. Not as catchy as the 'Misery Index' developed during the last big bout of stagflation in the U.S. in the late 1970s, but a metric to keep an eye on.

Mr. Zandi was hardly the only pundit in the Stagflation corner last week. The IMF warned last week that the ongoing conflict in Iran will 'slow global growth, raise inflation, and worsen food insecurity globally.' Not to be outdone, the head of U.S. equity and quantitative strategy at BofA Securities noted that current market indicators pointed to a 'classic stagflationary market environment.'

Investors are already seeing the early signs of an inflationary surge. Friday's CPI reading rose 0.9% on a month-over-month basis in March. This was up from 0.3% in February. March's CPI level was up 3.3% over the prior 12 months. This was up significantly from 2.4% in February. Gasoline prices surged just over 21% during the month, which accounted for nearly three-quarters of the CPI increase from February.

Diesel prices have risen more than 50% so far in 2026. That increases logistical costs across the entire economy, as trucks and trains use diesel to move goods to market. The price of jet fuel in the U.S. has also roughly doubled. It has moved from $2.50 a gallon on Feb. 27 to $4.88 a gallon on April 2. It has fallen back a bit since then. United Airlines (UAL) and other airlines have raised fees to help offset costs and have started to raise fares. A jet fuel shortage could trigger mass cancellations in Europe if not relieved over the next three weeks.

In summary, I view last week's rise as little more than a short-lived relief rally. The probabilities of new hostilities breaking out in the Middle East by the end of April appear to be at least 50/50. The chances of the Strait of Hormuz being fully reopened are much, much lower than that. Simply put, this isn't over yet. Nowhere close, in fact. And the longer this global chokepoint remains effectively closed, the greater the likelihood of recession or stagflation.

Neither scenario is properly priced into the markets. Like most investors, I was happy to see green across most of my portfolio last week after some rough going for equities in March. That said, I did little trading within my portfolio. I rolled some of my covered call holdings forward and added a bit to my gold mining positions. At the end of the week, my portfolio was roughly 30% in short-term treasuries and cash and 70% in covered call holdings around the few stocks in the market I see as attractively or reasonably priced. If hostilities renew in the weeks ahead, there will be ample opportunities to use some of that 'dry powder' to act upon lower entry points in the market.
